observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City strictly comply with the requirements of Section 37 of RA No. 9003 to convert open dumpsites into controlled dumpsites. In addition, the City must expedite the drafting of changes in the design of the SLF and allocate funds for the project to hasten the construction of a sanitary landfill as final disposal of residual wastes so that the City can adopt a systematic, comprehensive and ecological wastes management program and encourage waste reduction/reuse/recycling and composting as strategies to reduce the solid waste.
observations.managementAction:
Ongoing implementation of the various programs, innovations, and strategies relative to waste reduction, reuse and recycling of solid wastes.
CENRO SLF to be operational by end of 2024. Requir ements of Sec. 37 of RA 9003 at the SLF is ongoing which is now 80% fully completed. The implement admin Bldg. is finished and the installation of the HDPE liner in the cell as per ECC will soon commence in close coordination with the DENR.
Awaiting for the re-ordered HDPE liner to conform as the first liner that was delivered by the supplier was rejected by the DENR.
2.Bio-waste shredders and plastic shredders are all operational and being utilized. Shredded bio-wastes are used as substance for the vermin-composting at the city nurseries and demo farms. Other urbanized barangays are also encouraged to do the vermin-composting like Brgy. Jonobjonob.
3.Shredded plastic residuals are diverted using the DOST technology of mixing with asphalt for the maintenance of city streets as well as additives to the ongoing foot walk fabrication using Class “A” design mix. Per batch of foot walk fabrication., 10 – kls of shredded plastic residuals are mixed with 10 bags cement, 20 bags sand and 30 bags gravel producing 37 pcs. Concrete foot walk measuring 60-cm x 90 cm, 3” thickness. This wastes diversion strategy for residuals together with asphalt mixing considerably reduced the city’s plastic residual wastes to as much as 30% based on CENRO data.
4.Campaign for eco-bricking of plastic residuals in the different government offices and barangays is ongoing. Brgy. Rizal is utilizing their eco-bricks in their plant boxes, gardens and MRF wallings. CENRO is using the same as ornamental plant boxes.
5.Recovering recyclables intensified by having a MOA with a licensed junkshops operators and organizing the city garbage sorters into an association.
6.Launch a massive plastic waste recovery program in partnership with the academe and other stakeholders. All types of plastic wastes recovered are delivered to a recycling facility, SUBAY MARKETING in EB Magalona where the LGU forged a MOA.
7.The implementation of various environmental ordinances such as Ord. 139-Waste Segregation Ordinance of the City intensified by imposing the non-collection of non-segregated wastes from household \s and institutions and giving disciplinary actions such as work suspension to CENRO personnel in cases of violations of work instructions.
8.The total ban on the use of styro as food containers, etc. had also bear results. CENRO is deploying Eco-enforcers constantly checking establishments and the city public market for violators and citation tickets are issued. Campaign for the full implementation in the different barangays using the social media and IEC during barangay assemblies.
9.Non-collection of bio-wastes from households since composting is encouraged at the household level.

We recommended that the City Mayor discontinue the practice of procuring goods, supplies, materials and other requirements of the City’s program, projects and activities through cash advance since it is not one of the modes of procurement prescribed by RA 9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act. 

We also recommended that the City adhere strictly on the provisions of RA 9184 in all its procurement of goods, infrastructure projects and consulting services to ensure transparent, competitive, efficient and effective method.

We recommended that the City Mayor promptly designate a competent In-charge of Office, who possesses the qualifications provided in the Treasury Manual, in case a vacancy in the Office of the City Treasurer will transpire in the future to have a smooth transition of duties and responsibilities including accountability of the outgoing officer to the incoming OIC-City Treasurer.

It is recommended that the city government designate an Assistant City Treasurer or an alternate employee that will perform the functions of the City Treasurer in his absence, in order to comply with the provisions of Section 14, Book I of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.

It is recommended that the City Hall be manned with adequate security personnel to ensure the safety and security of officials, employees and clients. This would lessen the possibility of untoward events that may occur within the compound. It is likewise recommended that the building should be given more maintenance work especially sanitation and pest control. We also recommend that the aforementioned premises within the City Treasurer’s Office be properly secured with adequate facilities so as to minimize the risk of untoward incidents. The people who go in and out should also be strictly monitored.

We recommend that the LGU Escalante City implements the minimum employees net take home pay of four thousand pesos (P4,000.00) monthly. The OIC-accountant shall direct payroll clerks to observe the limitations set and the order of preference imposed in authorized deductions as prescribed in the General Appropriations Act of 2013.

Impose the withholding of salary and the institution of other sanctions as provided under Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated                           February 10, 1997.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City Mayor create an inventory committee composed of the representative from the City Mayor’s Office, General Services Office and the Accounting Office to undertake the physical count of inventories and PPEs.
He must immediately direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of inventories and PPEs and to submit the RPCI/RPCPPE as soon as the conduct of inventory is completed.
The Accounting and GSO undertake a regular reconciliation of their records in order to have a fair presentation of the inventory and PPE accounts in the financial statements.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor strictly adhere to the regulation that only permanently appointed officials shall be designated as disbursing officer pursuant to 4.1.4 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
The City Treasurer immediately transfer the disbursing function to Ms. Despi to a permanent employee under his Office.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Local School Board (CLSB) comply with the provision of Section 363 of the Local Government Code or RA 7160 in the prioritization of programs, activities and projects that should be included in the School Board Budget.
Moreover, the CLSB strictly comply with the latest revised guidelines on the use of SEF as prescribed under the Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017, specifically items 4.0 and 5.0 thereof.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor and the OIC-City Accountant require the DOs and SDOs to immediately submit their Liquidation Reports and supporting documents to settle the unliquidated amount of ₱12,465,864.45.
The OIC-City Accountant strictly comply with the regulation that no additional cash advance shall be granted unless the previous one has been liquidated or a proper accounting thereof has been made pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of P.D. 1445.
Henceforth, advances to officers and employees must be liquidated within the prescribed period and closed at year-end, pursuant to 5.8 of COA Circular 97-002.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City appropriate 1% of its annual Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) for the strengthening and implementation of the programs of the LCPC pursuant to Section 15 of RA 9344.
In case the appropriation for the LCPC is sourced from the 20% Development Fund, the projects and program must follow the guidelines in the utilization of the 20% Development Fund provided under JC No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017.
Work and Financial Plan must be prepared as a guide on the projects, activities and programs to be implemented during the year and as a gauge that the desired objectives of LCPC has been achieved.
Lastly, the City must follow the guidelines in the identification of program, projects and activities to be charged against the 1% LCPC under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor discontinue the practice of procuring goods, supplies, materials and other requirements of the City’s program, projects and activities through cash advance since it is not one of the modes of procurement prescribed by RA 9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act.
We also recommended that the City adhere strictly on the provisions of RA 9184 in all its procurement of goods, infrastructure projects and consulting services to ensure transparent, competitive, efficient and effective method.
Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor stop approving expenses pertaining to religious or other private activities and no public funds shall be spent for religious purposes pursuant to Section 335 of RA 7160.
We also recommended that the OIC-Accountant strictly evaluate festival expenses of the City and ensure that disbursement of government funds adhere strictly to laws, rules and regulations, specifically Section 4 of PD No. 1445 or the fundamental principles governing financial transactions and operations of government agency.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the OIC-City Accountant or the City Treasurer submit within seven days after release of check, the cash advance voucher with covering transmittal letter enumerating the supporting documents in a sealed envelope to the COA Team pursuant to                     JC No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City OIC-City Accountant immediately reconcile the loans payable account on the City Coliseum and the 13.5 Farm to Market Roads project with the LBP-Bacolod Branch in order to present accurate and reliable loan balances in the financial statement.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Government strictly comply with the provisions of Republic Act 9003, especially with the minimum requirements for the operation of a controlled dumpsite pursuant to Section 37 thereof until such time that a land fill is constructed.
We also recommended that the City expedite the construction of landfill so it could comply with the requirements of R.A. 9003 in its ecological solid waste management.
Lastly, we recommended that the City strictly implement the “No Segregation, No Collection” Ordinance and promote among its residents proper garbage segregation.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The OIC-City Accountant compute for the depreciation of the DVGDH building from the time the said property was recorded in the books in order to have a fair presentation of the said account in the financial statement.
The City Mayor direct the General Service Officer (GSO) and   the City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO) to exert effort in locating the necessary documents relative to the construction and ownership of DVGDH.
The City Mayor send a communication to the Provincial Government to determine if said property was recorded or not in the books of the Province and also to ascertain its ownership.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Treasurer immediately transfer the bank balance of ₱7,084,423.34 with the BDO to any authorized government depository banks pursuant to DOF Circular No. 01-2017 dated May 11, 2017.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the OIC-City Accountant expedite the preparation of the BRS and submit as soon as possible all overdue BRS so that the City officials and other users of the report will be informed of the status of the bank accounts and in case of errors between the balance per books and the bank, they could be detected at once.
The OIC-City Accountant furnish the Audit Team a copy of the BRS on time so the Team could conduct timely verification of the report. In order to ensure timely submission of BRS, the OIC-City Accountant should make representations with the depository bank to furnish the City, copies of the bank statements at the earliest possible time.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Treasurer direct all AOs/DOs to faithfully adhere to Sections 28 and 54 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II by requiring them to maintain a cashbook to record all transactions affecting their cash advance and to prepare the RD to account for cash disbursements out of their cash advances received within the prescribed period or after the purpose of the cash advance has been served and submit the report together with the DVs and supporting documents to the City Accounting Office.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Development Council (LDC) adhere to the updated guidelines in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF pursuant to DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
We also recommended that the City Mayor ensure that the 20% DF is optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the City.
Moreover, the OIC-City Accountant and the City Budget Officer ascertain proper charging of expenses to the 20% DF.

      guidelines issued by DILG and DBM under JMC No. 2011-1 dated April 13,
      2011 in prioritizing projects  for  inclusion  in  the  City’s  Annual
      Investment Plan or 20% IRA for developmental projects.


           The City Accountant and the City Budget  Officer  ensure  proper
      charging  of  expenses  to  the  20%  Development  Fund   and   submit
      justification  for  aforesaid  payments  which  were  charged  to  20%
      Development Fund.

Recommendation:

           We recommended that the City Government faithfully  comply  with
      the guidelines, policies and procedures  in  the  utilization  of  the
      LDRRMF pursuant to NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 26,
      2013.

Recommendation

           We recommended that the City Local  School  Board  refrain  from
      charging non-related expenses to the SEF and strictly  adhere  to  the
      regulation on the  prioritization  of  expenses  charged  against  the
      School Board Fund as provided under Article 363 (c) of the IRR  of  RA
      7160 and Section 4 of DECS-DBM-DILG JC No. 01  series  of  1998  dated
      April 14, 1998, and likewise faithfully comply with  Article  184  (3)
      (b) (2) of IRR of RA 7160.

Recommendation:

           We recommended that the City Mayor submit  immediately  specific
      legal basis for granting Achievement Incentive to all  City  officials
      and employees. Otherwise, he should  direct  all  City  officials  and
      employees  to  return  the  amount  received  considering  that   said
      disbursements has no legal basis.

Recommendation:

           We recommended  that  the  City  Mayor  faithfully  comply  with
      qualification  standards  whenever  personnel  matters  are  involved,
      especially when there is movement (e.g. hiring, promotion,  etc.)  and
      ensure that the provisions  of  Section  22,  Personnel  Policies  and
      Standards of Chapter  5,  Revised  Administrative  Code  of  1987  are
      strictly adhered to every time the City implements  its  own  manpower
      complement or organizational structure.

Recommendation:

           We  recommended  that  the  City  Mayor  promptly  designate   a
      competent  In-charge  of  Office,  who  possesses  the  qualifications
      provided in the Treasury Manual, in case a vacancy in  the  Office  of
      the City Treasurer will transpire in  the  future  to  have  a  smooth
      transition of duties and responsibilities including accountability  of
      the outgoing officer to the incoming OIC-City Treasurer.

Recommendation:

           We  recommended  that  the  City   Mayor   instruct   the   City
      Administrator’s Office to design a comprehensive security plan for the
      City, employ trained security personnel or provide continuous training
      to hired security personnel to ensure that they are ready  to  respond
      to crimes that may occur such as, robbery and vandalism and to  ensure
      the safety and security of officials, employees  and  clients.  It  is
      also recommended that the premises within the City Treasurer’s  Office
      be properly secured to protect the cash and other  valuable  documents
      therein.


           The security personnel must be instructed  to  strictly  monitor
      and inspect people who go in and out  of  the  premises  in  order  to
      lessen the possibility of untoward incidents that may occur within the
      compound.


           Additionally, it was recommended that the City Hall be  provided
      with  sufficient  maintenance  work  especially  sanitation  and  pest
      control to make the facility fit for a good working place.
